data.title: Marvell Stock Leads 3 AI Infrastructure Picks Tied To US Chip Spending
data.description:
Intel’s latest earnings put a spotlight on how quickly money is moving toward AI hardware and the factories that support it. With Q2 revenue up 25% year on year, data centre and AI sales at $6.3b, and 2024 capital spending guidance lifted to $20b, investors are reconsidering which U.S. industrial and semiconductor related stocks might benefit or face tougher competition. This article looks at 3 stocks from a U.S. Industrial Reshoring and Semiconductor Manufacturing screener that appear positively exposed to the same forces driving Intel’s AI and foundry ambitions, helping you decide which stories deserve a closer look.

Advertisement

Marvell Technology (MRVL)

Overview: Marvell Technology designs and sells advanced data infrastructure chips that move and process data inside AI data centers and high speed networks, covering everything from custom accelerators to optical interconnects and storage controllers for customers around the world.

Operations: Marvell generates about US$8.7b a year from designing, developing and selling integrated circuits, with revenue spread across China (US$3.3b), other international markets (US$2.5b), Taiwan (US$1.8b) and the United States (US$1.0b).

Market Cap: US$183.4b

Intel’s acceleration in AI and foundry spending shines a light on Marvell Technology because it supplies the high speed custom silicon and optical links that help power those same AI data centers, while also attracting heavyweight partners like Nvidia and large cloud providers. The appeal is a mix of strong AI driven revenue momentum, expanding roles in custom AI chips and interconnects, and forecasts for earnings and revenue growth well ahead of the broader U.S. market. However, investors also need to weigh a rich valuation, heavy reliance on hyperscaler capex and some customer concentration and funding risks. The full story of how those trade offs stack up against Intel’s spending burst is where things get really interesting.

Marvell’s AI momentum and custom chip partnerships are impressive, but the real story may be how its earnings and revenue forecasts compare with that rich valuation. For more detail, review the analyst forecasts for Marvell Technology

Micron Technology (MU)

Overview: Micron Technology is a global memory and storage specialist, supplying DRAM, high bandwidth memory, NAND flash and solid state drives that sit at the core of AI data centers, PCs, smartphones, cars and industrial devices under the Micron and Crucial brands.

Operations: Micron generates most of its revenue from memory focused business units, with around US$31.3b from the Cloud Memory Business Unit, US$27.2b from the Mobile and Client Business Unit, US$21.2b from the Core Data Center Business Unit, US$10.5b from the Automotive and Embedded Business Unit and US$19m from All Other segments.

Market Cap: US$1,083.6b

Micron Technology is closely linked to the AI build out that Intel’s results highlight, supplying the DRAM, high bandwidth memory and storage that give AI servers their capacity while expanding U.S. manufacturing that aligns with onshoring and CHIPS Act priorities. Multi year customer contracts covering around one fifth of DRAM and a third of NAND volumes are intended to smooth out memory cycles and support margins, and recent results show earnings, revenue and profit margins at very strong levels. At the same time, investors may wish to stay alert to classic memory risks, including heavy capital spending needs, high non cash earnings, insider selling signals and intense competition from Asian peers. The key consideration is how those factors balance out against today’s valuation and AI expectations.

Micron’s AI memory surge and U.S. reshoring push are only half the story, and the real tension sits between that enthusiasm and today’s pricing. Get the full picture in the analysis report for Micron Technology.

Lattice Semiconductor (LSCC)

Overview: Lattice Semiconductor develops low power field programmable gate arrays, or FPGAs, and related software that let customers reconfigure chips for tasks such as data center control, industrial automation, automotive systems and edge AI without having to redesign the hardware.

Operations: Lattice Semiconductor generates about US$574.0m in revenue from its Core Lattice segment, with sales spread across Greater China (US$325.0m), the Americas (US$91.6m), Europe including Africa (US$73.9m), Other Asia (US$53.1m) and Japan (US$18.0m).

Market Cap: US$18.9b

Intel’s AI driven spending surge puts Lattice Semiconductor in focus because its low power FPGAs often sit alongside the big accelerators as “companion chips”, managing control, security and data movement in servers, networking gear and industrial systems. Analysts have highlighted expectations for rapid earnings and revenue growth and point to recent awards in cybersecurity and edge AI, plus design wins with hyperscalers, as factors that support this view. At the same time, the stock trades on a very high P/S multiple, current margins are modest and there has been meaningful insider selling and new debt to help fund the planned AMI deal. The tension between that rich valuation and the AI plus reshoring opportunity is where the most important questions for investors sit.

AI plus reshoring could be accelerating Lattice Semiconductor’s story, but the real twist is how its rich P/S, margins and insider moves fit together in the 1 key reward and 2 important warning signs

data.title: Eli Lilly (LLY) Stock Still Looks Cheap Following Its 407% Run
data.description:
Eli Lilly stock has returned roughly 4x over the past five years, yet the current valuation picture is split, with the intrinsic value estimate pointing to meaningful upside while the broader checks suggest the shares are not an obvious bargain.

  • Eli Lilly has delivered a very large 5 year return of about 406.7%, which puts extra scrutiny on whether recent gains already reflect the key growth drivers.
  • Expectations around new therapies in obesity, neuroscience and oncology can support optimistic cash flow assumptions. However, legal disputes and execution risk on acquisitions such as AtaiBeckley may weigh on how much investors are willing to pay for that growth.
  • The Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) work implies the stock trades about 27.9% below intrinsic value. In contrast, Eli Lilly only passes 2 of 6 valuation checks on Simply Wall St, which points to a company that screens as expensive on many metrics despite the DCF upside.

The issue now is whether Eli Lilly's current share price already reflects most of its growth story or still leaves a reasonable margin versus the intrinsic value estimate.

Eli Lilly delivered 48.3% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Pharmaceuticals industry.

Advertisement

Is Eli Lilly a Bargain on Cash Flow?

The Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model estimates what Eli Lilly is worth today based on the cash it is expected to generate for shareholders. Eli Lilly currently produces around $8.6b in free cash flow over the latest twelve months, and the DCF uses a growing cash flow path that reaches higher projected levels by 2030.

On this basis, the model points to an intrinsic value of about $1,644 per share. This figure sits above the current share price and implies the stock screens as undervalued by roughly 27.9%. Despite recent headlines around the AtaiBeckley acquisition, the DCF output suggests the market is not fully pricing in the cash flows implied by Eli Lilly’s obesity, neuroscience and oncology pipelines.

Overall, Eli Lilly stock appears undervalued relative to the intrinsic value indicated by the DCF cash flow projections.

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Eli Lilly is undervalued by 27.9%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 38 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Eli Lilly.

Does Eli Lilly Look Fairly Valued on Earnings?

P/E is a useful lens for Eli Lilly because earnings are a central yardstick for established pharmaceuticals. Eli Lilly trades on a P/E of about 41.8x, which is much higher than the Pharmaceuticals industry average of 15.0x and also above the peer group average of 25.5x, so the stock clearly carries a premium to many large drug companies.

The fair P/E ratio implied by the model is 39.2x, which reflects what investors might expect to pay given Eli Lilly’s size, margins, growth profile and risks. The current 41.8x multiple sits only slightly above that fair ratio, suggesting the market is pricing in most of the company’s strengths without stretching too far beyond the model’s benchmark.

Overall, Eli Lilly appears roughly fairly valued on its P/E multiple, trading at only a modest premium to the model’s fair ratio.

Instead of focusing on a single multiple or model, each Narrative lays out the key inputs behind its view of fair value so you can compare those assumptions with Eli Lilly's actual results over time.

The Eli Lilly debate splits sharply between investors who see years of GLP 1 fueled upside and those who think pricing and concentration risks already sit in the share price.

The Bottom Line

That tension, together with a low broader value check score, suggests the DCF optimism is offset by how much investors already pay for growth using simple ratios. The split largely comes down to whether Eli Lilly can translate its obesity, neuroscience and oncology pipelines into durable cash flows that justify both the DCF upside and the current premium P/E, or whether the market is correctly flagging legal and execution risks as a reason to be cautious on the valuation.

data.title: ON Semiconductor (ON) Stock Trades At A Premium To Fair Value
data.description:
ON Semiconductor stock has delivered a 133.4% return over the past five years, yet its current valuation checks suggest the shares may be trading at a premium to an estimate of intrinsic value based on a Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model and earnings multiples that both lean overvalued.

  • A 133.4% return over five years highlights that ON Semiconductor has already rewarded long term holders, which can lower the margin of safety for new investors if expectations are high.
  • Expectations for ON Semiconductor's future cash generation can support the current share price, but any disappointment in execution or profitability could weigh heavily on what already screens as a full valuation.
  • ON Semiconductor passes only 1 of 6 valuation checks, which points to a stock that currently looks more expensive than cheap on broad measures.

The issue now is whether ON Semiconductor's current share price already reflects most of its intrinsic value, or if there is still enough upside potential to justify paying what looks like a premium.

ON Semiconductor delivered 62.6% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Semiconductor industry.

Advertisement

Does ON Semiconductor Look Pricey on Cash Flow?

The Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) approach here values ON Semiconductor by estimating the cash it may generate for shareholders over time. Based on this model, the latest twelve month free cash flow sits at about $745 million, with analysts and extensions of those forecasts assuming growing cash flows over the coming years rather than a shrinking profile.

Running those projections through the 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model produces an estimated intrinsic value of about $75 per share. Compared with the current share price, this implies the stock trades at roughly a 19.7% premium to that DCF estimate. This suggests a market price that already builds in optimistic expectations around ON Semiconductor’s future cash generation.

On this DCF view, ON Semiconductor stock currently screens as overvalued relative to its modeled intrinsic value.

The P/E ratio is a useful way to see how much investors are paying for each dollar of ON Semiconductor's earnings. ON Semiconductor currently trades on a P/E of about 61.1x, which is close to the wider semiconductor industry average of 60.4x.

However, the tailored fair P/E ratio from this model is 54.6x, which is lower than where ON Semiconductor trades today. That gap suggests the stock is pricing in richer expectations than the model implies based on factors such as the company’s growth outlook, profitability, size, and risk profile.

Five Year 14.4% Earnings Growth Meets 0.7% Slowdown

  • Over the last five years, B-R 31 Ice CreamLtd grew earnings at an average of 14.4% per year, but the latest trailing 12 month earnings increase was 0.7%, and Q2 2026 trailing EPS of ¥172.58 is close to the ¥171.44 to ¥189.50 range seen over the past five trailing 12 month snapshots.
  • Supporters of a bullish view often see the 14.4% multi year growth as proof of a strong track record, yet the most recent 0.7% earnings growth rate and a net profit margin now at 4.5% instead of 5.1% challenge a simple growth story by showing that the past pace has not continued in the latest year.
    • One way to look at it is that trailing 12 month net income moved from ¥1,652 million a year ago to ¥1,663 million now, which is an increase in yen terms but a very small step compared with the earlier five year trend.
    • At the same time, Q2 2026 EPS of ¥73.89 sits within a pattern of quarterly swings, from a loss per share in Q4 2025 to higher figures like ¥93.09 in Q3 2025, so the long run growth history has come with meaningful ups and downs along the way.

P/E of 23.5x and Price Above DCF Fair Value

    • On the cautious side, the premium to the industry average and the gap between ¥4,060 and the DCF fair value mean the valuation could be sensitive if expectations for cash flows or margins shift.

data.description:
Nuclear energy stocks are back in focus as investors weigh stubborn inflation signals, rising government bond yields and renewed attention on reliable power sources. With oil prices influenced by geopolitical tensions and energy feeding directly into inflation worries, interest in long term, steady electricity supply is building. The Nuclear Energy Stocks screener highlights companies across uranium production, fuel enrichment and reactor operations that sit at the heart of this theme. In this article, you will see three stocks from the screener that offer different ways to gain exposure to nuclear energy as a potential part of a diversified portfolio.

Advertisement

Worley (ASX:WOR)

Overview: Worley is a Sydney based engineering and professional services company that helps energy, chemicals and resources clients worldwide design, build, operate and eventually decommission large projects, including growing work in low carbon energy, nuclear power, hydrogen and carbon capture.

Operations: Worley reports A$12.4b from segment adjustments and related items, with geographic exposure spread across the Americas (A$6.2b), Europe, the Middle East and Africa (A$4.0b) and Australia, Pacific, Asia and China (A$1.4b).

Market Cap: A$5.2b

Worley provides exposure to the energy transition through consulting and project work that already skews toward sustainability, with management indicating 60% of FY25 revenue is expected from areas like renewables, hydrogen and carbon capture. Analysts currently expect earnings to grow faster than revenue as the mix shifts toward higher margin advisory and digital services. At the same time, the stock trades on a modest P/E against peers and internal fair value estimates. The catch is that margins are thin, earnings fell over the past year and funding leans on higher risk borrowing, so execution on backlog quality and cost discipline will matter. The balance between transition exposure, valuation considerations and these risks may be a key focus for investors reviewing Worley.

Worley’s push into higher margin transition work may be masking a deeper shift in the business model, and the valuation gap to peers could matter less than what happens to margins next. It is therefore worth reading the 3 key rewards and 1 important warning sign

Boss Energy (ASX:BOE)

Overview: Boss Energy is an Australian uranium company focused on bringing its Honeymoon project in South Australia into production and growing output through a 30% stake in the Alta Mesa project in South Texas. This gives investors direct exposure to physical uranium production across two jurisdictions.

Market Cap: A$568.8m

Boss Energy attracts attention because it is working to lower operating costs at Honeymoon through a new wellfield design and process tweaks. The company holds 1.62 million pounds of drummed uranium inventory and a largely uncontracted future sales book that leaves revenue closely tied to uranium prices. Analysts currently expect strong growth in revenue and earnings as production scales and margins improve, although the company is not yet profitable and has historically reported losses. The balance sheet is cash rich and debt free, which can support drilling and feasibility work. Upcoming production decisions and project milestones mean this is a uranium producer where both upside and key risks are closely linked to how these projects and prices evolve.

Boss Energy’s accelerating uranium story, with cash on hand and projects gearing up, still leaves one big question about how the growth path and pricing risks really fit together. This is unpacked in the analyst forecasts for Boss Energy

Peninsula Energy (ASX:PEN)

Overview: Peninsula Energy is an Australian uranium company working to develop the Lance Project in Wyoming, a group of production, development and exploration areas that aim to supply uranium into the nuclear fuel chain for US and global utilities.

Market Cap: A$148.6m

Peninsula Energy catches attention because the stock is priced at a steep discount to some fair value estimates and trades at a low P/B multiple, yet the business is still small, loss making and reliant on higher risk borrowing. Forecasts indicate the possibility of higher revenue and earnings if Lance scales up. However, recent production guidance changes, rising losses over the last five years and fresh equity issuance highlight the challenges that remain. With a new but independent board and a volatile share price, this is a uranium developer where potential upside sits alongside funding pressure and execution risk, which makes understanding the full risk reward trade off critical before forming a view.

Sharplink currently trades on a P/S ratio of 29.7x, which stands out when you set it against its recent share price weakness and long term return profile.

Against that backdrop, Sharplink screens as expensive in several ways. Its 29.7x P/S is well above the US Hospitality industry average of 1.7x and also above the peer group average of 1x. Tega Industries (NSEI:TEGA)

Overview: Tega Industries is a Kolkata based manufacturer of specialized equipment and wear resistant consumables used in grinding, screening, and material handling for mining and mineral processing operations worldwide, serving customers across North and South America, Europe, Africa, Asia, Australia, and India. Its portfolio spans mill liners, hydrocyclones, conveyor components, and wear lining systems that help mines keep throughput high and downtime under control.

Tega Industries catches investors’ attention because it combines a global consumables heavy business that can create recurring demand with an internal DCF estimate that sits well above the current share price, even after a year where net income and margins came under pressure. The company is tying its growth to rising mining activity and stricter ESG standards, where its wear resistant and sustainability oriented products aim to support customers’ cost and compliance needs. M & B Engineering (NSEI:MBEL)

Operations: M & B Engineering generates its revenue primarily from pre engineered buildings, structural steels, steel roofing, and related components, with this segment contributing ₹12,597.22 million.

• Oracle, ticker NYSE:ORCL, has secured a nearly $7b, 10 year software contract with the U.S. Department of Defense.
  • The agreement will be used to modernize the Pentagon's defense technology stack and support cloud and AI adoption.
  • The contract is expected to cover a wide range of critical military and intelligence organizations across the defense community.

For investors tracking Oracle, this new Pentagon contract arrives after a challenging share price stretch. NYSE:ORCL last closed at $120.04, with the stock down 27.3% over the past month and down 38.7% year to date. Over a longer horizon, the stock is up 6.8% over three years and 46.5% over five years, which gives useful context for the recent pullback.

The size and duration of this contract indicate that Oracle may play a central role in the Department of Defense's long term IT modernization. For your watchlist, key questions include how quickly this agreement converts into recognized revenue and whether it leads to additional awards across U.S. and allied government agencies.
